title: Memory Boxes - How Computers Remember Things
description: Students learn variables as memory containers and track how values update through sequences, loops, and conditionals.
learningObjectives: Explain what a variable is and why computers need variables.
Track variable value changes step by step while a program runs.
Write simple variable updates using assign and update actions.
Connect variables to loops and conditionals from previous lessons.
prerequisites: Builds on Lessons 1-3: sequences, loops, and conditionals.
materials: One small cardboard box or envelope per group, or drawn boxes on board
Sticky notes or small paper pieces for variable values
fullDescription: Age group: Groep 5-8 (ages 8-12). Duration: 45-60 minutes. Devices required: none (fully unplugged). SLO alignment: Domain A (21A), Domain B (22B). Students model program memory with named boxes and live value updates. Role model: Radia Perlman, whose networking algorithms use changing variables to keep internet paths stable and prevent loop issues.

teacherInstructions.setup: Prepare three variable boxes or board drawings labeled SCORE, LIVES, and LEVEL. Keep vocabulary visible all lesson: Variable, Value, Assign, Update, State.
steps: Phase 1 (0-3 min): Spark question on how games remember progress after power-off. Capture ideas and introduce variables as runtime memory containers.
Phase 2 (3-10 min): Box game warm-up. Update SCORE, LIVES, LEVEL events one by one and repeatedly ask for full state after each update.
Phase 3 (10-20 min): Concept introduction with box metaphor. Teach number/text/Boolean variable types and connect to loops and conditionals.
Phase 4 (20-42 min): Arcade scoreboard challenge. Track SCORE, SHOTS_LEFT, MULTIPLIER during 10 shots, then debug divergence between groups. Add even SHOTS_LEFT conditional for round 2.
Phase 5 (42-47 min): Discussion on variable design choices, constants versus changing variables, and data ethics prompts by age level.
Exercises (47-57 min): Complete Exercise 1 (Track the variables) and Exercise 2 (Design the virtual pet). Use vending machine extension for fast finishers.
Share and reflect (52-57 min): Pairs share one variable and one conditional. Class spots missing cases and completes metaphor sentence for variable understanding.
discussionPrompts: If SCORE reset each level but LIVES carried over, how would game feel different?
Which variable was most critical: SCORE, SHOTS_LEFT, or MULTIPLIER? What breaks if removed?
Can a value that never changes still be a variable, or is it something else?
What rules should apps follow if they track user mood-like behavior variables?
tips: Acknowledge storage hardware answers, then focus on in-program variable memory.
After each event, always ask for full state, not only changed variable.
Make Lesson 3 link explicit: conditionals read variable values to choose branches.
For ages 8-10 simplify scoreboard to SCORE and SHOTS_LEFT only.
For ages 10-12 keep multiplier and conditional bonus rule for higher cognitive load.

teacherGuide: Core message: variables are program memory and state changes over time. Students should demonstrate reliable state tracking across updates, loops, and condition branches. Look for students who identify cascade errors after a missed update, since this mirrors real software debugging.

studentExercises.title: Exercise 1 - Track the Variables
studentExercises.description: Read the given program line by line and record HEALTH, SHIELD, and POTIONS after every step.
instructions: Start values: HEALTH = 100, SHIELD = false, POTIONS = 3.
Step 1: character walks into fire -> subtract 20 from HEALTH.
Step 2: character picks up a shield -> set SHIELD to true.
Step 3: character walks into fire again -> IF SHIELD = true THEN subtract 0 from HEALTH ELSE subtract 20.
Step 4: character uses a potion -> add 30 to HEALTH and subtract 1 from POTIONS.
Step 5: character walks into fire again -> IF SHIELD = true THEN subtract 0 ELSE subtract 20.
Write the full state after each step and compare with a partner line by line.
successCriteria: Student tracks all three variables at every step.
Condition branches in Steps 3 and 5 use SHIELD correctly.
Final state matches: HEALTH 110, SHIELD true, POTIONS 2.

studentExercises.title: Exercise 2 - Design the Virtual Pet
studentExercises.description: In pairs, define a 3-variable pet system with events and conditionals that make variables interact.
instructions: Define exactly three variables with starting values (for example HUNGER, HAPPINESS, ENERGY).
Write three events that change the variables.
Write two conditionals that use those variables.
Ensure at least one event changes more than one variable.
Swap with another pair and test whether rules produce believable pet behavior.
successCriteria: Three variables and start values are clearly defined.

studentExercises.title: Extension - The Broken Vending Machine
studentExercises.description: Track vending-machine variables through transactions and debug edge cases including change handling and state reset bugs.
instructions: Use start variables: STOCK_CRISPS 5, STOCK_WATER 0, STOCK_CHOCOLATE 3, COINS_INSERTED 0, CHANGE_DUE 0.
Process customer inserting 2.00 and selecting crisps (1.50). Write every variable update step.
Handle edge cases: selecting out-of-stock water, no change available, selecting chocolate with insufficient funds.
Simulate three customers and write final values of all five variables.
Challenge: explain what happens if COINS_INSERTED is not reset to 0 after purchase.
successCriteria: Transaction updates are complete and ordered.
At least two edge cases are handled with explicit rules.
Student explains session-state persistence bug consequences.

studentChallenges.title: Arcade Scoreboard Race
studentChallenges.description: Track SCORE, SHOTS_LEFT, and MULTIPLIER in real time while shots are taken, then debug mismatch points across groups.
materials: Paper balls and bin or floor target
instructions: Start with SCORE = 0, SHOTS_LEFT = 10, MULTIPLIER = 1.
For each shot: decrease SHOTS_LEFT by 1.
If shot goes in: add 10 to SCORE, then multiply by MULTIPLIER.
After every 3 successful shots in a row: increase MULTIPLIER by 1.
Compare final scores across groups and trace divergence back to first incorrect update.
successCriteria: Group tracks all three variables every shot.
Multiplier updates occur at correct streak moments.
Group can locate first error when totals diverge.

studentChallenges.title: State Inspector
studentChallenges.description: Add an even/odd conditional to SHOTS_LEFT and correctly apply bonus logic before score updates.
instructions: Before each shot, check: IF SHOTS_LEFT is even THEN shot is worth double points if it goes in ELSE normal points.
Run five additional shots.
Track both the conditional decision and resulting variable updates each shot.
Compare results with another group and debug differences.
Explain one case where wrong condition timing causes wrong score.
successCriteria: Conditional is evaluated before scoring event.
Even-shot bonus is applied only when condition is true.
Group can justify scoring sequence with state evidence.
